Name: dynamic_kernel_fusion
Title: Dynamic Kernel Fusion for Hardware-Optimized Inference of Quantized Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: Can we enhance inference speed and efficiency of quantized neural networks by implementing dynamic kernel fusion strategies tailored to specific hardware profiles, leading to reduced memory bandwidth requirements and improved throughput?
Related Work: Existing literature has explored various quantization methods, such as adaptive and training-free approaches (e.g., AMP-ViT and EMQ). However, these methods do not sufficiently consider the real-time adaptation of kernel execution based on hardware-specific characteristics. The proposed research will distinguish itself by focusing on dynamic kernel fusion, which involves combining multiple operations into a single kernel tailored for a specific quantization scheme and hardware profile, thereby directly addressing hardware resource utilization.
Abstract: As machine learning models continue to grow in complexity, their deployment on hardware with limited resources necessitates innovative optimization strategies. This proposal presents a novel approach to enhance the efficiency of quantized neural networks by implementing a dynamic kernel fusion strategy that adjusts kernel execution based on real-time hardware profiling. By analyzing hardware-specific metrics such as memory bandwidth usage and latency, we aim to fuse operations within a neural network layer to minimize memory accesses and maximize throughput. The approach will leverage dynamic profiling during inference to select and optimize kernel configurations for specific hardware characteristics, significantly reducing the overhead associated with multiple kernel launches. The effectiveness of this dynamic fusion will be validated through rigorous benchmarking on various quantization levels and model architectures, focusing on real-world hardware measurements. Expected outcomes include improved inference latency and energy efficiency, contributing to the deployment of efficient AI solutions on resource-constrained devices.
Experiments: 1. Dynamic Kernel Fusion Implementation: Modify the existing CUDA/Triton kernels to enable dynamic kernel fusion based on hardware profiling data. 2. Profiling Metrics: Collect metrics such as achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and latency under different quantization settings. 3. Benchmarking: Compare the performance of the dynamic kernel fusion approach against standard kernel execution across various quantization levels (e.g., 4-bit, 8-bit). 4. Evaluation Metrics: Measure wall-clock latency, tokens-per-second throughput, and energy consumption per inference to assess improvements.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Hardware Dependency: The performance gains may vary significantly across different hardware configurations; thus, the findings might not generalize well. 2. Complexity in Implementation: The dynamic kernel fusion mechanism may introduce additional complexity that could affect maintainability and reproducibility. 3. Limited Application Scope: The proposed method may perform better on certain types of neural networks (e.g., convolutional vs. recurrent networks), which could limit its applicability.
